feature.name: overscroll-behavior: chain
feature.rolloutPlan: Will ship enabled for all users

intake.finding: CSS keyword value addition completing the 2x2 matrix of overscroll-behavior effects (scroll propagation x local border effect); the missing 4th combination 'chain' = propagation + no local effect. Concrete use case: side menus/drawers that should not bounce/stretch but still chain scroll to an ancestor. Owner vmpstr@ (Blink>Scroll); incubated in CSSWG (issue #13370 opened 2026-01-19). Mode correctly diagnosed as a standards-track CSS value, not a Chrome-only API.
intake.limitations: No end-user or stakeholder scan; the problem is framed entirely from a developer use case.

incubation.finding: Incubated in the correct venue (CSSWG). Issue #13370 (2026-01-19) was placed on the CSSWG spring 2026 face-to-face agenda (Agenda+ FTF), resolved, and the spec edited via PR #13861; the name evolved from the author's suggested 'forward'/'continue' to the adopted 'chain'. The explainer states the one alternative considered (do nothing / browser default) and rejects it as low-cost; a live demo is provided.
incubation.limitations: No independent developer research or customer/partner evidence; TAG later noted the explainer 'assumes a lot of knowledge' and lacks accessibility/i18n sections.

prototype.finding: Implementation landed in Chromium (crbug 499018879; chromium-review 7832830, reviewed by flackr@). WPT PR #59792 (merged 2026-05-11) adds parsing tests (valid/invalid/computed) and behavioral tests across wheel, keyboard, and gesture inputs on both X and Y axes, plus code fixes so tests pass, and explicitly 'moves overscroll-behavior: chain to experimental'. Intent to Prototype was sent to blink-dev.
prototype.limitations: crbug body not retrievable (issues.chromium.org returned HTTP 500 on multiple attempts); no task-based friction logs or framework-integration evidence (low need for a single keyword value).

developerTrials.finding: ChromeStatus carries a DevTrial stage (130 / intent_stage 2) but devtrial_instructions and flag_name are null, and no evidence of a structured developer-trial cohort, recruitment, or attributable feedback exists. The path appears to have been runtime-flag -> experimental (per WPT PR) -> ship without a published trial.
developerTrials.limitations: Absence of evidence rather than evidence of absence; for a small additive value a formal trial may be disproportionate, but it is undocumented either way.

wideReview.finding: CSSWG resolved (issue closed via spec PR; value normatively in the editors' draft). Mozilla standards-position #1406 is labeled 'position: positive'. TAG review #1228 was discussed across six meetings (2026-05-18 to 2026-07-06) and resolved 'satisfied with concerns' on 2026-07-16 with two material concerns: (1) the explainer is missing accessibility/i18n/impact sections, and (2) the name 'chain' is confusing vs 'auto' and may violate the CSS value-naming convention (values are usually adjectives). WebKit #661 remains Open with no position. Critically, the TAG's written resolution (2026-07-16) post-dates Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30), so the value shipped before wide review formally closed and before the naming concern was delivered in writing.
wideReview.limitations: Exact Mozilla position-close date unknown (ChromeStatus still showed 'No signal' as of 2026-06-15); TAG meeting notes are paraphrased summaries.

experiment.finding: An Origin Trial stage exists in ChromeStatus (150 / intent_stage 3) but every OT field is null - no trial was configured or run. For a low-risk additive keyword value completing an existing Baseline property, no field experiment is warranted: there is no compat risk and no privacy/security surface beyond the existing overscroll-behavior property (spec confirms 'no new abilities beyond preventDefault').

prepareToShip.finding: Intent to Ship was sent (confirmed). Rollout is enabled-by-default (finch CSSOverscrollBehaviorChain) on desktop/Android/WebView M150 (no iOS), with enterprise_impact none, breaking_change false, and activation/security/ergonomics risks recorded as 'None'. The spec includes 'chain'. Chrome release comms were prepared (beta blog, release notes). Gaps at ship: ChromeStatus still showed tag/security/privacy review 'Pending' and Mozilla 'No signal' as of accurate_as_of 2026-06-15 (stale vs Mozilla's actual 'positive' and the active TAG review); MDN was not yet updated with 'chain'; and the value shipped before the TAG review was resolved.
prepareToShip.limitations: Intent-to-Ship full body not retrievable (msgid URL rejected by the reader; confirmed via search snippet only).

release.finding: Chrome 150 was promoted to stable on 2026-06-30; 'overscroll-behavior: chain' shipped enabled-by-default on desktop/Android/WebView. It is covered in the Chrome 150 beta blog (2026-06-02) and the Chrome 150 release notes. The feature is live in stable as of the evidence cutoff (~3 weeks old).
release.limitations: Only ~3 weeks post-stable; no post-ship telemetry, regression, or incident data is available yet.

adoption.finding: Adoption of the 'chain' value is unknown (it has shipped for under one month). Context: the base overscroll-behavior property is Baseline / widely available across all engines since September 2022 and is well-documented and discussed (MDN, CSS-Tricks, Ahmad Shadeed, Ben Nadel, Stack Overflow questions, and a Figma feature request for scroll-chaining control), indicating a mature, understood property - but no Chrome use-counter or adoption data specific to the 'chain' keyword was found.
adoption.limitations: No chain-specific usage metric was retrievable.

support.finding: The feature is too new for public support issues, known regressions, or community Q&A specific to 'chain' to have surfaced; there is no support-burden evidence in either direction.
support.limitations: No support evidence found; feature is <1 month in stable.

deprecation.finding: This is a new additive CSS keyword value, not a restriction, replacement, or removal; no migration, detection tooling, removal timeline, or reverse trial applies.

developerValue.finding: A real, narrow, standards-aligned problem: it completes the 2x2 overscroll-behavior effect matrix (propagation x local effect) and unblocks drawer/menu UX plus the sibling 'Overscroll Gestures' feature (per the ChromeStatus web-dev note). Mozilla and the TAG both 'see the benefit for web authors'. However, the chosen name 'chain' was flagged by TAG as confusing relative to 'auto' and possibly non-conformant with the CSS value-naming convention, and the explainer is too thin to communicate the value/impact to unfamiliar reviewers. Net: value is real, naming/communication quality is mixed.
developerValue.limitations: No direct developer-demand research; demand inferred from the use case and an adjacent feature.

endUserImpact.finding: The effect is a subtle UX polish (suppress local bounce/affordance while preserving scroll chaining) on drawer/menu components; TAG noted a possible cognitive-accessibility benefit but also flagged RTL/i18n interaction as uncertain. No user research was performed (TAG recorded 'User research: N/A') and no end-user impact data exists.

adoption.finding: No chain-specific adoption is measurable at under one month in stable; only base-property adoption context exists.

interoperability.finding: Positive standards trajectory: 'chain' is normatively in the CSSWG editors' draft, Mozilla's position is 'positive', and the TAG is 'satisfied'. But as of the cutoff only Chromium ships 'chain' (WebKit has no position; it is not Baseline for 'chain'), the TAG review was not formally closed at ship time, and a naming concern remains open. MDN/caniuse are not yet updated to include 'chain'.
interoperability.limitations: Spec is an editors' draft (not yet CR); single-engine implementation.

implementationQuality.finding: Strong, specific implementation+test evidence: WPT PR #59792 (merged 2026-05-11) adds parsing tests (valid/invalid/computed) and behavioral tests across wheel, keyboard, and gesture inputs on both X and Y axes, with code fixes made so tests pass; the implementation was reviewed by flackr@; it is a low-risk additive change to an existing property.
implementationQuality.limitations: crbug body not retrievable (host HTTP 500); no post-ship regression/performance data yet.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Process evidence is coherent and Chrome-side release comms are good and on time (beta blog + release notes). But there are several accuracy/quality gaps: ChromeStatus review fields were stale at ship (Mozilla shown 'No signal' vs actual 'positive'; tag/security/privacy 'Pending' while the TAG actively reviewed); the explainer is too thin (TAG: missing accessibility/i18n); MDN was not updated with 'chain' at ship; and the value shipped before the TAG review was formally resolved.

support.finding: No support evidence is available; the feature is under one month in stable.

overall.finding: A fast, low-risk, standards-blessed addition to a Baseline property with strong test coverage and positive Mozilla/TAG signals - but it cannot be scored a success: it shipped before its TAG review formally closed with an unresolved naming concern, ChromeStatus review fields were stale, MDN was not updated, and adoption/end-user-impact/support remain unknown because the value is under one month old. Known dimensions are mixed; post-ship outcome dimensions are unscored.
overall.limitations: Overall is dominated by post-ship unknowns.

successes: Rapid, venue-correct CSSWG incubation: issue #13370 (2026-01-19) -> spring 2026 FTF resolution -> spec edited (PR #13861), with a concrete demo and an explicitly-considered alternative.
Comprehensive WPT coverage shipped alongside the implementation (parsing valid/invalid/computed + wheel/keyboard/gesture behavior on both axes), reviewed by flackr@ (PR #59792, merged 2026-05-11).
Low-risk, additive, non-breaking change to an already-Baseline property; clean enabled-by-default rollout (finch CSSOverscrollBehaviorChain) with no enterprise impact.
Positive Mozilla standards position; the TAG ultimately satisfied; timely Chrome 150 beta blog and release-notes coverage.
failures: Shipped in Chrome 150 stable (2026-06-30) before the TAG review was formally resolved (2026-07-16); the TAG's substantive naming concern ('chain' vs 'auto' confusing; departs from the CSS value-naming convention) was not addressed before developers could depend on the keyword - and CSS value names are effectively permanent.
Explainer too thin: no accessibility, internationalization, or considered-impacts sections (TAG flagged this); the Intent-to-Prototype thread even listed 'No information provided' for explainer/spec.

ChromeStatus review/browser fields were stale and inaccurate at ship (Mozilla 'No signal' vs actual 'positive'; tag/security/privacy 'Pending' despite active review), understating interoperability support in launch materials.
MDN (canonical reference) and caniuse were not updated with 'chain' at ship, leaving the value discoverable only via Chrome's own channels.
No developer-trial cohort, friction evidence, or post-ship adoption/support measurement is in place (the latter is partly a function of age).
counterfactuals: Had the team waited for (or pre-emptively chased) the TAG's written resolution before the M150 branch, the 'chain' naming question could have been settled with CSSWG/TAG before the keyword became permanent, avoiding a hard-to-reverse rename risk.

Had ChromeStatus browser-position/review fields been reconciled against the live Mozilla/WebKit/TAG issues within ~2 weeks of ship, launch materials would not have under-stated Mozilla support ('positive' shown as 'No signal').
Had an MDN/caniuse PR been filed in the same milestone as ship, developer discovery of the new value would be materially better at launch.

A short task-based friction check (a drawer component on Android + desktop, including RTL) would have pre-answered the i18n/cognitive-accessibility questions the TAG raised and produced reusable evidence rather than 'N/A'.
skillImprovements: Add a phase-04/06 hard-gate eval: for any new CSS keyword value, block the ship branch while its TAG review is open OR while value naming is contested; treat CSS value names as effectively immutable post-ship. Use overscroll-behavior: chain as the regression weak-case.

Add a release-readiness checklist item: reconcile ChromeStatus browser-position and review-status fields against the live Mozilla/WebKit/TAG issues within 14 days of ship; flag any 'Pending'/'No signal' that contradicts an actual resolved position.
Add a documentation-readiness gate for CSS value additions: require a filed/merged MDN and caniuse update in the same milestone as ship.

Add an explainer-quality regression eval enforcing accessibility, i18n, security, privacy, and alternatives sections (explicit 'none' allowed) per repeated TAG feedback; weak case = this feature's explainer.
Add a phase-02 prompt to generate a one-task friction log (including RTL and mobile) for even 'trivial' CSS additions, so user-impact questions have evidence rather than 'N/A'.

missingEvidence: crbug 499018879 body (issues.chromium.org returned HTTP 500 on multiple read attempts) - implementation/bug detail unverified.
Intent to Ship (blink-dev) full body - msgid URL rejected by the reader; existence/content confirmed only via a search snippet (LGTMs and exact review states at ship not captured).
Chrome use-counter / adoption metric for the 'chain' keyword specifically.
Post-ship regression, performance, and support telemetry (the feature is under one month in stable).
Exact date Mozilla applied/closed its 'position: positive' label, to pin interop-support timing relative to ship.
